items.front: What must be included when clearing an arriving aircraft to a clearance limit?
items.back: Issue the fix or airport, route including STAR or transition if used, altitude or descend-via instructions, any required holding or delay information, and further communication instructions.

items.front: How is advance descent handled near an adjacent center boundary?
items.back: Coordinate for the lower altitude with the receiving facility and initiate the descent far enough out to allow normal descent and speed reduction.

items.front: When does a single-frequency approach (SFA) keep military turbojets on the same frequency?
items.back: Do not require a frequency change after the approach begins or after initial en route-descent contact until landing or low approach is complete, except for listed visual, pilot-request, cancellation, or emergency cases.

items.front: What radio or beacon change rule applies to military single-piloted turbojets in IFR conditions or at night?
items.back: Avoid changes as much as traffic permits, make them early, minimize them below 2,500 feet AGL, avoid them in turns, and do not require them during a missed approach before missed-approach altitude, MEA, or MVA.

items.front: What special information must be given before a military turbojet descends below the highest high-altitude approach initial-fix altitude?
items.back: Advise the expected approach type, that radar vectors to final will be provided, and weather if below the stated ceiling or visibility limits. Issue descent early enough for the normal high-rate profile.

items.front: What arrival information must en route control forward to towers, FSSs, or approach control?
items.back: Forward the aircraft ID, type, ETA or actual time, intended procedure or SVFR or contact-approach details, and for approach control any clearance limit, EFC, and transfer point information required.

items.front: When must en route controllers issue weather to arriving aircraft?
items.back: Before or with the approach clearance when official weather is below the stated ceiling or visibility thresholds and the report is passed directly or relayed through a noncarrier station or nonapproach facility.

items.front: What if an arriving aircraft reports weather below its landing minima?
items.back: Issue instructions to hold or proceed elsewhere and adjust the landing sequence or approach clearances of other aircraft as needed.

items.front: How early must jurisdiction be transferred on arrival?
items.back: Transfer communications and control early enough for the receiving facility to clear the aircraft beyond the clearance limit before it reaches that point.

items.front: What approach information must be given on initial contact or as soon as possible thereafter?
items.back: Issue the approach or expected approach, runway if different, wind, below-threshold ceiling or visibility, altimeter, special weather changes, and any needed advisory about unavailable ILS or runway transition.

items.front: What arrival information must an approach control facility forward to towers or centers?
items.back: Forward aircraft ID, type, ETA, approach type, SVFR or contact-approach details to towers or FSSs, and to centers send the expected procedure, holding and EFC data, missed approaches, and overdue-aircraft information.

items.front: What airport-condition information must be issued before an approach or as conditions change?
items.back: Inform aircraft of abnormal approach-aid operation and destination conditions that could restrict approach or landing, including RwyCC and, where applicable, RCR information.

items.front: What must be done when switching from one ILS runway to another where multiple systems are not used simultaneously?
items.back: Coordinate the change with facilities that use fixes formed by reference to those NAVAIDs.
